threats.title: sqrt(0) Executes STOP Opcode Halting Entire Calling Transaction
threats.hypothesis_description: The sqrt function contains `stop()` in assembly for zero input instead of returning packedFloat(0). The STOP opcode halts the entire EVM execution context with success status but no return data. Any calling contract computing sqrt(0) will have its transaction silently terminated mid-execution, committing only partial state changes. Subsequent operations (balance updates, event emissions, return values) are never executed.
threats.investigation_rationale: Any DeFi protocol computing sqrt of a value that can reach zero (depleted AMM reserves, zero variance, zero collateral) will experience silent transaction corruption. The success status means the caller receives no error, but all operations after the sqrt call are dropped.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Assembly block: `if iszero(a) { stop() }` — uses STOP opcode instead of returning zero or reverting

preconditions: Any contract calls Float128.sqrt with packedFloat.wrap(0) as input
The calling transaction has subsequent operations after the sqrt call

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: stop() opcode terminates entire execution frame silently with success, unlike revert which rolls back
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any caller triggering sqrt(0) is affected
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always exploitable when sqrt input is zero

operational_questions: Which upstream protocols call sqrt and can their inputs reach zero?
What partial state changes get committed when execution halts?
Can an attacker force an input to zero to halt a liquidation or withdrawal mid-execution?
validation_requirements: Call sqrt(packedFloat.wrap(0)) from a contract with state changes before and after
Verify success status returned with empty returndata
Confirm post-sqrt state changes are dropped while pre-sqrt changes persist

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify protocol that calls Float128.sqrt in a multi-step state-changing function
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manipulate on-chain state so that the sqrt input evaluates to zero
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction executes partially, committing partial state but dropping remainder
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol state is now inconsistent (e.g., tokens debited but shares not minted)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete function-level DoS; partial state corruption in calling contracts
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Depends on which state changes were committed vs dropped
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any protocol operation using sqrt where input can be zero

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — trigger sqrt(0) through normal protocol interaction
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time the sqrt input can be zero

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Transactions that succeed but produce no return data from sqrt calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Inconsistent state where half of a multi-step operation completed
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated attempts to deplete reserves or create zero-value conditions

threats.title: ln() Silently Accepts Negative and Zero Inputs Returning Incorrect Results
threats.hypothesis_description: The ln() function does not validate the sign bit of its input. For negative inputs, it silently computes ln(|x|) instead of reverting. For zero input, instead of reverting (ln(0)=-infinity), it falls into the Taylor series branch where mantissa=0 causes z_int=10^76, computing a finite incorrect result (~3.32 in a scaled form). This masks upstream errors and produces plausible-looking but completely wrong values in pricing and interest calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike sqrt() which explicitly checks for negative inputs and reverts, ln() performs no sign validation. Any protocol using ln() for interest rates, bonding curves, or logarithmic pricing will silently get wrong results if a negative or zero intermediate value reaches ln().

proof_of_signal.pattern: mantissa extracted via `and(input, MANTISSA_MASK)` which ignores sign bit; no check for `and(input, MANTISSA_SIGN_MASK)` before computation. Compare with sqrt() which has explicit sign check and revert.

preconditions: Protocol passes a negative or zero packedFloat to Ln.ln()
Upstream code does not pre-validate positivity before calling ln()

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Sign bit at position 240 is within MANTISSA_MASK boundary but extracted separately; ln ignores it entirely
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always exploitable when ln receives negative or zero input

operational_questions: What specific value does ln(0) return? Trace full computation path.
Does ln(-x) return exactly ln(x) or a different wrong value?
Which DeFi formulas using ln() can produce negative intermediates?
validation_requirements: Call Ln.ln(Float128.toPackedFloat(-5, 0)) and verify it returns ln(5) instead of reverting
Call Ln.ln(packedFloat.wrap(0)) and verify it returns a non-zero finite value
Compare ln(negative) output with ln(abs(negative)) to confirm they match

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify protocol using ln() in pricing/interest formula
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manipulate state so ln() receives negative value (e.g., from subtraction that goes negative)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: ln() returns plausible but incorrect positive result
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Incorrect result propagates through formula, enabling favorable pricing/interest extraction

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Silent miscalculation in any protocol using ln() for pricing or rates
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Difficult — errors compound through subsequent calculations
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Logarithmic bonding curves, interest rate models, pricing formulas

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate — understand protocol's use of ln()
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When protocol state can produce negative/zero ln input

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpected pricing outputs from protocols using ln()
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Interest rates or prices that don't match expected mathematical behavior

threats.title: le() and ge() Return False for Mathematically Equal Values with Different M/L Encodings
threats.hypothesis_description: The le() and ge() comparison functions use strict lt/gt for the equal-exponent-equal-sign case after normalizing both operands to L-size. When two values are mathematically equal but encoded differently (M vs L), the early bitwise equality check fails (different bit patterns), and the body uses `lt(aMan, bMan)` instead of `iszero(gt(aMan, bMan))`. This means le(a,b) returns false when a==b mathematically. Combined with eq()'s bitwise-only comparison (OPS-H004), trichotomy is broken: a value can be simultaneously not-less-than, not-equal-to, and not-greater-than another equal value.
threats.investigation_rationale: DeFi protocols using le/ge for threshold checks (liquidation ratios, collateral requirements, fee caps) will have boundary conditions fail. A position exactly at the liquidation threshold could escape detection.

proof_of_signal.pattern: In le(), both-positive same-exponent branch: `retVal := lt(aMan, bMan)` — strict less-than instead of less-than-or-equal. Same pattern in ge() at lines 504-508.

preconditions: Two packedFloats represent the same mathematical value
One is M-encoded (38 digits) and the other L-encoded (72 digits)
After L-normalization, their mantissas and exponents are numerically identical

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: le/ge body uses strict lt/gt comparisons; equality only caught by bitwise check which fails for M/L differences
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever arithmetic produces L-encoded result compared to M-encoded threshold

operational_questions: Which specific values can exist in both M and L form through normal operations?
Does the MAXIMUM_EXPONENT autoscaling prevent some M/L collisions?
Can an attacker force a computed value to be L when the threshold is M?
validation_requirements: Create M and L representations of the same value via toPackedFloat
Verify le(M_val, L_val) returns false when values are equal
Verify ge(L_val, M_val) returns false when values are equal
Confirm trichotomy violation: !lt && !eq && !gt for equal M/L pair

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify protocol threshold stored as M-encoded packedFloat
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Perform operations that produce L-encoded result equal to threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: le/ge check fails at exact boundary, bypassing threshold enforcement
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Position at exactly-at-threshold escapes liquidation or safety check

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Threshold checks silently fail at boundary values
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All protocol checks using le/ge for collateral ratios, caps, or limits

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When computed value exactly equals threshold

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Positions at exact thresholds that are not flagged

threats.title: eq() Bitwise Comparison Fails for Same Mathematical Value in M vs L Encoding
threats.hypothesis_description: Float128.eq() compares raw uint256 bit patterns only: `packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)`. The same mathematical value encoded in M-size (38 digits) vs L-size (72 digits) has different bit patterns (different mantissa, different exponent, different L-flag). eq() returns false for mathematically equal values with different encodings, breaking equality semantics.
threats.investigation_rationale: Equality checks are used for exact-match comparisons in protocols (identity verification, state machine transitions, expected-value assertions). Silent false negatives allow bypassing checks that should match.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `retVal = packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)` — pure bitwise comparison with no mathematical normalization or M/L-aware comparison

preconditions: Two packedFloats represent the same mathematical value
They are encoded in different precision modes (one M, one L)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: eq() has no normalization logic; all other comparison functions at least attempt L-normalization
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever M and L representations of equal values are compared

operational_questions: Can toPackedFloat produce both M and L for the same value with different inputs?
Which arithmetic operations naturally produce L-encoded results for values that would normally be M?
validation_requirements: Create value 1.0 in both M and L forms
Verify eq() returns false
Demonstrate specific arithmetic sequences producing M/L divergence

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify protocol using eq() for exact-match checks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Construct computation path producing L-encoded version of expected M value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: eq() check fails, bypassing the matching logic
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol enters unexpected state (missed match, bypassed invariant)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Silent equality failures in all downstream protocols
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Moderate — requires fixing eq() to normalize before comparison
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any protocol check using Float128.eq for value matching

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: State machine transitions not triggered at expected values

threats.title: EVM exp() Overflow Corrupts add/sub Results for Exponent Differences ≥82 (L-size)
threats.hypothesis_description: In add() and sub(), when aligning mantissas for operands with different exponents, the code uses `exp(BASE, adj)` in assembly to compute 10^adj. The EVM EXP opcode computes modulo 2^256. For adj ≥ 78 (10^78 > 2^256), the result wraps to an arbitrary non-zero value. The subsequent `sdiv(bMan, wrappedValue)` produces a large garbage value instead of the mathematically correct ~0, corrupting the addition result. For L-size operands, this triggers at exponent difference ≥ 82; for M-size, at ≥ 116.
threats.investigation_rationale: DeFi protocols combining values of vastly different magnitudes (e.g., total supply + dust, collateral value + tiny fee) within a single formula will get corrupted results. The documented supported range is ±3000, so differences of 82+ are well within bounds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `bMan := sdiv(bMan, exp(BASE, adj))` — when adj ≥ 78, exp(10, adj) wraps mod 2^256 to arbitrary non-zero value; sdiv by wrapped value produces garbage. Same pattern at lines 95-101, 120-130, etc.

preconditions: Two packedFloat operands with effective exponent difference ≥ 82 (L) or ≥ 116 (M)
Both operands are within the documented valid range [-3000, +3000]
The smaller operand does not align via the multiplication branch (neg=false)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: EVM `exp` opcode wraps for base=10, exponent≥78, but code does not clamp adj or check for overflow
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — triggered by any caller with valid inputs
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever two values with large magnitude difference are added/subtracted

operational_questions: What specific wrapped values does exp(10, adj) produce for adj in [78, 255]?
What is the resulting corruption magnitude for realistic DeFi number pairs?
Can the corruption be steered to produce a specific target value?
validation_requirements: Compute add(toPackedFloat(1, 50), toPackedFloat(1, -40)) and verify result correctness
Compute add(toPackedFloat(1, 100), toPackedFloat(1, -100)) and verify corruption
Map the adj threshold where exp first overflows for base 10

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify protocol formula that adds values of different magnitudes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Provide inputs creating ≥82 orders of magnitude difference
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Corrupted addition result propagates through protocol logic
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Extract value based on incorrect pricing/accounting

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Difficult — corruption may not be detected until funds are extracted
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any multi-precision calculation combining large and small values

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When protocol handles values with large magnitude differences

threats.title: Multiplication Exponent Overflow Produces Wrong Order-of-Magnitude Results
threats.hypothesis_description: In mul(), the result exponent is computed as `rExp = shr(EXPONENT_BIT, aExp) + shr(EXPONENT_BIT, bExp) - ZERO_OFFSET`. When both operands have large positive exponents, the sum can exceed 16383 (14-bit max). When packed via `shl(242, rExp)`, only the lower 14 bits are stored, causing silent wraparound. Example: multiplying two numbers with effective exponent +4100 each produces raw exponent sum 24574, which wraps to 8190 (decoded as -2), making the result ~10^8200 orders of magnitude wrong.
threats.investigation_rationale: While extreme exponents are documented as unsupported above ±3000, the library does not enforce these bounds. Any protocol allowing user-specified values could hit this boundary, producing results that appear valid but have completely wrong magnitude.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `rExp := sub(add(shr(EXPONENT_BIT, aExp), shr(EXPONENT_BIT, bExp)), ZERO_OFFSET)` — unsigned arithmetic with no overflow check; result packed into 14-bit field via shl(242, rExp)

preconditions: Two operands with large positive exponents (sum of decoded exponents > 8191)
No input validation on exponent range in calling protocol
Combined result exceeds the 14-bit exponent field capacity

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: 14-bit exponent field can only store [0, 16383]; mul produces results that can exceed this range
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When multiplying numbers with large exponents

operational_questions: At what exact exponent combinations does overflow occur?
What wrapped exponent value results from specific overflow scenarios?
Can the documentation's ±3000 limit be enforced programmatically?
validation_requirements: Compute mul(toPackedFloat(1, 4100), toPackedFloat(1, 4100))
Verify the result exponent wraps and decode the incorrect value
Test boundary: mul with exponents summing to exactly 8191 vs 8192

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Supply very large exponent values to a protocol using mul()
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Multiplication produces result with wrapped exponent
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Result appears valid but has completely wrong magnitude
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Extract value from protocol using the incorrect result

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Result magnitude off by thousands of orders of magnitude

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Values changing magnitude unexpectedly after multiplication

threats.title: Unsigned Exponent Subtraction Underflow in add/sub for Small Raw Exponents
threats.hypothesis_description: In add() and sub(), the initial exponent adjustment `r := sub(aExp, shl(EXPONENT_BIT, MAX_DIGITS_M))` performs unsigned subtraction. When the raw packed exponent field value is less than 38 (MAX_DIGITS_M) — corresponding to effective exponent below approximately -8154 — this subtraction wraps around to a massive uint256 value. The subsequent normalization and exponent adjustments operate on this corrupted exponent, producing completely wrong results. The same pattern exists for L-size numbers with DIGIT_DIFF_76_L.
threats.investigation_rationale: While extreme exponents are flagged in documentation, the code does not enforce bounds. Very small numbers from deeply nested calculations (compound interest over many periods, repeated multiplications of numbers < 1) could reach these ranges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `r := sub(aExp, shl(EXPONENT_BIT, MAX_DIGITS_M))` — unsigned subtraction wraps when rawExp < 38, producing ~2^256 exponent value

preconditions: At least one operand has raw exponent field < 38 (effective exponent < -8154 for M)
Numbers produced through repeated multiplication of small values or direct construction
The two operands have different exponents (triggering the alignment code)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assembly `sub` is unsigned; no check that packed exponent field ≥ MAX_DIGITS_M before subtraction
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When very small numbers (effective exponent < -8154) are in add/sub

operational_questions: Can normal arithmetic sequences produce values with effective exponent < -8154?
What specific corrupted values result from the underflow?
Does the normalization step accidentally correct any underflow cases?
validation_requirements: Create packedFloat with effective exponent -8155 via toPackedFloat
Add it to another small number and verify result correctness
Trace the wrapped exponent value through the normalization path

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Construct or discover protocol state producing very small floating-point values
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Trigger add/sub operation with the extreme value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exponent underflow produces massive incorrect result

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Silently corrupted arithmetic at extreme ranges

threats.title: Multiplication Exponent Underflow for Very Small Numbers
threats.hypothesis_description: In mul(), `rExp = shr(EXPONENT_BIT, aExp) + shr(EXPONENT_BIT, bExp) - ZERO_OFFSET` underflows when both raw exponents are small (e.g., both near 0, representing very negative effective exponents). If raw_aExp + raw_bExp < 8192, the subtraction wraps to ~2^256. The result is packed with this massive exponent, producing a value orders of magnitude too large instead of the correct very small product.
threats.investigation_rationale: Multiplying two very small numbers should produce an even smaller number. Instead, the wrapped exponent makes the result appear enormous. This inverts the expected mathematical relationship, potentially allowing value extraction from protocols using multiplication for scaling down.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `rExp := sub(add(shr(EXPONENT_BIT, aExp), shr(EXPONENT_BIT, bExp)), ZERO_OFFSET)` — unsigned subtraction wraps when sum of raw exponents < 8192

preconditions: Both operands have effective exponents summing below -8192 (raw exponent sum < 8192)
Values within the documented ±3000 range: e.g., both with exponent -2000

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No underflow guard on exponent subtraction in multiplication
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When multiplying two numbers with strongly negative exponents

operational_questions: At what exact exponent combination does underflow occur?
Does the subsequent normalization accidentally mask the underflow?
Can this be triggered through realistic DeFi arithmetic chains?
validation_requirements: Compute mul(toPackedFloat(1, -2000), toPackedFloat(1, -2000))
Verify the result exponent and compare to expected -4000
Test boundary at effective exponent sum = -8192

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create or discover two small values in protocol state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Multiply them to trigger exponent underflow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Result appears as a very large number instead of very small
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Use inflated result to extract disproportionate value

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Products of small numbers become large — inverts mathematical expectations
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Multiplication of values with negative exponents

threats.title: Division Exponent Underflow for Extreme Quotient Ranges
threats.hypothesis_description: In div(), the non-L path computes `rExp := sub(add(aExp, ZERO_OFFSET), bExp)` in assembly. When dividing a small number by a large number, aExp is small and bExp is large. After aExp is decremented by MAX_DIGITS_M (which may already underflow), adding ZERO_OFFSET may not be sufficient to prevent the final subtraction from underflowing. The L path uses Solidity `unchecked` arithmetic with similar risks. The resulting wrapped exponent makes the quotient appear as a huge number instead of a tiny one.
threats.investigation_rationale: Division of extreme value ratios (small/large) produces results with extreme negative exponents that overflow the representation. The `unchecked` block explicitly suppresses the overflow check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: L-path: `unchecked { rExp = (aExp + ZERO_OFFSET) - bExp; }` — explicit unchecked arithmetic allows silent underflow

preconditions: Numerator has very small effective exponent
Denominator has very large effective exponent
Combined exponent range exceeds representable bounds
Values within documented ±3000 range can trigger this

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Explicit `unchecked` block wrapping exponent computation; assembly path also has no guards
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When dividing very small by very large numbers

operational_questions: At what quotient exponent range does underflow occur?
Does the non-L assembly path underflow differently from the L unchecked path?
What specific result values are produced from the underflow?
validation_requirements: Compute div(toPackedFloat(1, -3000), toPackedFloat(1, 3000))
Verify result exponent and compare to expected -6000
Map the boundary where underflow first occurs

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify protocol performing division with extreme value ratios
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Quotient has wrapped exponent appearing enormous
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Use inflated quotient in downstream calculations

potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Division operations at extreme value ratios

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Division results with unexpected large magnitude
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Ratios that should be tiny appearing large

threats.title: sqrt() Heron's Method Branch Exponent Underflow for Small Positive Numbers
threats.hypothesis_description: In sqrt()'s Heron's method branch (the else branch for small exponents), the assembly code computes `aExp := sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` or `sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M_PLUS_1)` to scale the mantissa. When the raw exponent (aExp) is less than MAX_DIGITS_M (38), this unsigned subtraction wraps to a massive value. The subsequent `div(sub(aExp, ZERO_OFFSET), 2)` performs unsigned division on the wrapped value, producing a huge exponent in the result. The square root of a very small positive number becomes a very large number.
threats.investigation_rationale: For numbers with effective exponent below approximately -8154, the sqrt function's Heron branch produces results with completely wrong magnitude. While extreme, this range is within the library's representable exponent range.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `x := mul(aMan, BASE_TO_THE_MAX_DIGITS_M); aExp := sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` — unsigned sub wraps when rawExp < 38; then `aExp := add(div(sub(aExp, ZERO_OFFSET), 2), ZERO_OFFSET)` uses unsigned div on wrapped value

preconditions: Input is a very small positive number with raw exponent < 38
Input does not trigger the large-exponent branch (first if condition)
Effective exponent approximately < -8154 for M-size inputs

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assembly `sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` has no underflow guard; unsigned division of wrapped value produces incorrect halved exponent
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When computing square root of very small positive numbers

operational_questions: What exact exponent range triggers the Heron branch vs the 512-bit branch?
Does the 512-bit branch have the same underflow issue?
What specific incorrect result magnitude does the wrapped exponent produce?
validation_requirements: Compute sqrt(toPackedFloat(1, -8160)) and verify result
Compare result to expected value (should be ~10^-4080)
Identify the exact branch boundary condition

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Push value below the critical exponent threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Use inflated result in downstream protocol logic

potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: AMM liquidity calculations, volatility computations using sqrt

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When protocol computes sqrt of very small values
